title: Will Colombia elect a far-right president?
gist: Colombia's 21 June runoff between Petro-backed leftist Iván Cepeda and Trump-endorsed outsider Abelardo de la Espriella (currently leading in polls) is framed as a test case for a broader Latin American shift toward Trump/Milei/Bukele-style populist outsiders, with US mining and oil interests, not either candidate's stated solutions, as the real stakes.

insights.claim: Colombia's presidential runoff on 21 June 2026 pits Iván Cepeda, leader of the Petro-endorsed left-wing Pacto Histórico coalition, against Abelardo de la Espriella, a businessman-lawyer with no prior political experience who is endorsed by Donald Trump and currently leads the polls.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Sets the base case that Colombia is on track to elect a Trump-aligned, business-friendly outsider rather than continue Petro's leftist agenda.
insights.locator: The second round of Colombia's presidential election

insights.claim: De la Espriella, nicknamed 'El Tigre,' blends the political style of Donald Trump, Argentina's Javier Milei and El Salvador's Nayib Bukele, presenting himself as a staunch anti-communist hardliner against organized crime, corruption and drug trafficking.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Positions Colombia's likely next president within a recognizable hemispheric political archetype (hardline, anti-establishment, US-aligned), useful for anticipating his likely governing style.

insights.claim: De la Espriella's platform includes shrinking the state, eliminating regulations, promoting mining and energy exploitation, cutting taxes on businesses and large fortunes, forcing banks to offer cheap home loans, and withdrawing Colombia from the UN and the Organization of American States.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A concrete policy platform with direct market implications: deregulation and mining/energy promotion would be bullish for extractive-sector investment in Colombia, while multilateral withdrawal signals a sharp foreign-policy break.
insights.locator: De la Espriella wants to reduce the size of the state

insights.claim: The author's own assessment is that neither candidate has real solutions to Colombia's core problems, despite representing opposite ends of the political spectrum.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A contrarian framing against both campaigns' own narratives — investors should discount both candidates' promises of a decisive fix to inequality, violence and territorial control.
insights.locator: gist framing in the raw article's own summary line

insights.claim: Cepeda's signature 'Paz Total' initiative to negotiate with non-state armed and criminal groups, and his separate attempt to reach a peace deal with the ELN guerrilla group, both failed while he ran them under Petro's government, and he is accused of indirectly helping armed groups gain ground.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A concrete governing-record liability for Cepeda heading into the runoff, relevant to assessing the credibility of his security policy promises if elected.
insights.locator: In Gustavo Petro's current government, Cepeda led

insights.claim: De la Espriella built his career as a defence lawyer for individuals linked to organized crime and paramilitarism, including Alex Saab, an alleged organized-crime operator who was both a close ally of Nicolás Maduro's government and reportedly a US DEA collaborator.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A reputational liability that could complicate international legitimacy for a de la Espriella presidency even among sympathetic diplomatic partners.
insights.locator: De la Espriella, meanwhile, became famous as a lawyer

insights.claim: Colombia's business community, most of the media, the international financial sector, and part of the armed forces would likely support a De la Espriella victory less out of enthusiasm for him personally than as a calculated bet on staying aligned with the Trump White House.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Suggests domestic elite backing for de la Espriella is transactional and US-alignment-driven, which could make that support fragile if Trump administration priorities shift.
insights.locator: The Colombian business community, most of the media

insights.claim: A De la Espriella presidency would fit Trump's 'Trump Corollary' Latin America security strategy, which seeks like-minded governments that cooperate on the war on crime and grant the US access to regional mineral and energy resources.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A de la Espriella win points toward expanded US access to Colombian mining and energy resources, directly relevant for US extractive and energy companies eyeing Colombia.
insights.locator: De la Espriella's approach fits in with Trump's national security strategy

insights.claim: If Cepeda wins instead, the Trump administration is expected to exert direct and indirect pressure through regional allies to limit his reforms, particularly tax reform and any new regulation of US mining and oil companies operating in Colombia.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: US corporate mining/oil interests in Colombia face political risk under a Cepeda win but would likely be actively defended by Washington pressure on Bogotá, partially offsetting that risk.

insights.claim: Ecuadorian President Daniel Noboa has explicitly backed De la Espriella in the Colombian election, prompting accusations of 'deliberate interference.'
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Shows Trump-aligned regional leaders actively coordinating cross-border political support, evidence of an emerging informal bloc of hemispheric right-populist governments.
insights.locator: as seen in Ecuadorian President Daniel Noboa's explicit backing

insights.claim: Colombia's election is part of a broader Latin American trend in which populist, non-political outsiders have surprised traditional right, left and centre parties by winning over large shares of voters by focusing on crime and nationalism versus multilateralism.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Frames Colombia as one data point in a hemispheric pattern investors should track across other upcoming Latin American elections.
insights.locator: This election is part of the wider trend

insights.claim: Cepeda has focused his campaign on denouncing De la Espriella's paramilitary links, but the author argues this misreads the nature of the 'new right,' which — as analyst Hernando Gómez Buendía put it — has not disappeared but 'changed hands' into a values-agnostic, results-focused pragmatism.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Explains why traditional left attacks on right-wing candidates' associations may be losing potency against a new brand of transactional, outcome-focused populism.
insights.quote: The right-wing did not disappear. It changed hands.
insights.locator: As Hernando Gómez Buendía, director of Razón Pública, points out
